---
title: "Data Cleaning, Recoding & Codebooks"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Novice]{.level-badge .level-novice} · **Data & Analysis** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Data & Analysis track](data-analysis.qmd){.track-back}

*Most analysis time is cleaning — let AI speed it up while you keep control.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Have AI flag data-quality issues and propose fixes
- Recode variables consistently and document the change
- Auto-draft a codebook and validate transformations

## Overview

Most of analysis is cleaning. AI can propose recodes, spot inconsistencies, and draft documentation — but every transformation needs a check that it did what you meant.

A **codebook** (what each variable means and how it's coded) is easy to skip and painful to lack. AI can draft one straight from your data, which you then correct.

## Practice activities

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 1 · Novice — Diagnose the mess

1. Paste a messy sample (inconsistent category labels, odd missing-value codes) into ChatGPT Edu.
2. Ask it to **list the data-quality issues** and propose a fix for each.
3. Decide which fixes to accept — you make the call, not the model.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Activity 2 · Novice — Recode and document

1. Ask AI to write code that **standardizes one variable** (e.g., unify category spellings).
2. Ask it to generate a **codebook entry** for that variable.
3. Run it, then **compare counts before and after** to confirm nothing was lost or mislabeled.
:::

## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "After an AI recode, what should you always check?", "options": ["The before/after counts and a few rows", "Nothing — AI is reliable", "The file size", "The chart colors"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Comparing counts and spot-checking rows confirms the recode did what you intended."}, {"q": "A codebook documents…", "options": ["What each variable means and how it's coded", "Your password", "The AI model version", "The file path"], "answer": 0, "explain": "A codebook is the map others (and future-you) need to use the data."}, {"q": "What data should you use for this practice?", "options": ["Public or synthetic", "Your real PHI dataset", "A colleague's grades", "IRB-protected records"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Keep practice data non-sensitive."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

From the AI readings library (ask the facilitator for access):

- **AI-Ready Data vs. Analytics-Ready Data** — why preparation determines whether analysis works.
- **Stop Learning Excel (Ruben Hassid)** — a provocative reframe of spreadsheet work with AI — read critically.

## Useful resources

- [tidyverse / dplyr](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org) — recoding and cleaning verbs in R.
- [OpenRefine](https://openrefine.org) — a free tool for cleaning messy tabular data.
- [Quarto](https://quarto.org) — document your cleaning steps reproducibly.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
Practice on **non-sensitive** data; de-identify before any tool sees real records. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
